preview: Two senior devs meet at the coffee machine. "Your code is wrong." "No, YOUR code is wrong." "You're using the wrong library." "No, YOU'RE using the wrong library." They're probably both right — and th

comments.text: That's not how senior devs talk. More like managers imagine, senior devs talk. Senior devs know, that there are different tools for different jobs, and there's no such thing as a "wrong library".

The difference between developer's idea of "can be changed in the future" and business' idea of it are that the developer values it more than the business values it. In fact, the business typically only values "solves the problem now". Maintainability is clawed back one enhancement crisis at a time. 

Look at how long it took the US auto industry to adopt quality as a real metric alongside quantity. We bought Japanese cars despite heavy tariffs, because they'd last as long as three cars from the the US big three. 

And then, when we do need to add a feature, we get told off by the PM and the Product owner for needing to do the refactoring that should have happened previously. Because refactoring isn't adding a new feature, and they have convenient amnesia regarding "We'll fix that later when we need to. Right now, we need to ship."

IMO "Can be changed in the future" leads to accumulating "Architectural debt", rarely addressed in the future until major issue emerges. Chicken or egg situation, has little impact on business unless risk can be estimated and proven quantitatively.
But the time for change can be from 15 minutes up to months in case code duplicates are used everywhere etc..
If one of those people is using Angular, one of them is definitely wrong.

AI-generated code is simply working code that works and is worth as much as the AI ​​model that generated it. It's simply another code producer in the code supply chain. An experienced engineer knows where and how to use it.

Been thinking this way for a while. Once you stop treating code like art and start treating it like a tool that has to work and stay flexible, most quality debates dissolve.
What's tricky tho is the cultural side. Some senior devs feel like their craft is being devaluated when you adopt this mindset. That tension is real and worth managing.
It shouldn't just run, 𝗶𝘁 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱𝘀 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲. Design principles are exactly what make that "𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗿" requirement actually possible.

Yitzhak Kornbluth As for AI-written code that can only be maintained by AI: Even assuming that the AI can in fact reliably maintain it (something that quite a few engineers say they've seen not happening), that locks you in to AI, which could be a problem if the venture capital dries up and the AI companies need to raise their prices to compensate.

Spot on! I’ve seen people doing refactoring to use new tools just because they are hyped to solve the problem better and faster. Conveniently ignoring man hours to get it done and failing to analyse what these changes will add to overall business value and propositions- the things really matter.

Software has this quality where it seems to work for the moment, but fails catastrophically without warning, given the right conditions. That failure can include function, performance, security, or even legal. Does this actually work, is it fast enough to compete, is it compatible with our security posture, can we afford licensing? It's a really low bar to simply argue efficacy.

AI code can certainly be modified later by AI. But i increasingly becomes a mess that eventually stops working. I am conducting an experiment rigth now. There is potential, that eventually it will actually be able to do what engineers do. But not yet.

Arguing at the coffee machine is often a sign of juniority. And when they understand your idea about the fact that code is not a holy grail but rather a business instrument, we call them "software developers". And when we can explain this idea to other developers, we call them "seniors."

Seniors would never use such language. Maybe you could hear it from juniors, and it is still a maybe.
A real senior would try to deal with this using constructive comments.  "Have you considered a different approach, maybe using this other library? Why would you prefer this one instead of the other?" Etc.

I have to disagree with your bolded statement. You named two criteria.
1. It solves the problem now.
2. It can be changed when requirements shift. Presumably you mean "easily changed".
I think you need to add:
3. It is relatively easy to maintain.

What makes code maintainable? There are quite a few factors, but one of the big ones is having clear invariants: Things that are required to be true, and that the entirety of the code can rely on. This makes it much easier to limit the possible edge cases, as well as providing natural approaches to tracking down bugs.

Of course, once you have an invariant, removing its status as an invariant is quite difficult, as you have to revisit all the code that relied on it. This creates a natural tension with criterion #2, and one of the main hard problems of software engineering is balancing these two goals of "can be changed when requirements shift" and "has enough stability to be maintainable". (How one balances these depends a lot on how the requirements might plausibly shift.)

Hi Peter Ovchynnikov

I agree with your point.

Though, maybe for the first time, there is one generally applicable design principle that is purely objective when evaluating the quality of a system/module/library/code construct: the 
hashtag
#IndependentVariationPrinciple (IVP).

The IVP won't always help you decide which tradeoff to make (though it helps for some), that's context-dependent.
But its existence means that identifying the best design, or the better design, is not anymore a matter of subjective preference and experience, but a matter of decisional authority structure in your organisation, and of which authority (team/unit/person) in your organisation decides when business rules, logic and concepts (the ubiquitous domain language) must change.

IVP paper: https://zenodo.org/records/18024111

I read this and think hmm might be spending the latter half of my career refactoring/rewriting AI created spaghetti code tightly coupled hairballs. Any decent size code base can be modified by AI until it can't and then good luck meeting business needs without spending money on human coders who will have to burn money to even get the code base back in a place where it can be modified to meet future business needs. I would be terrified if I had business mission critical code only written, understood and approved by an AI model. AI code has its place and can be a force multiplier and is a very powerful tool when used right (saying good bye to writing mindless boiler plate code which is exciting alone) but have a feeling a lot of enterprises are going to get a nasty lesson in the next few years how they misused generative AI to supposedly save money. Pound foolish easy trap to fall in. Works until it doesn't.

"Works on my machine" code works also. Should that now be acceptable in production too? Why not just eliminate all coding standards just because something works? The time spent writing proper code will be far lower than the time spent debugging, maintaining and ultimately refactoring poorly written code that introduces a lot of hidden nightmare scenarios. That's at the heart of the problem...you are asking to trade a small extra cost upfront to write good code with a large cost later of fixes, production issues and refactors. In a world of tradeoffs, that's an very, very bad decision. One that is not defensible.

Like "Functional Programming is better Class"...
Also, code might be written 1000 ways...

Yes maintentable code is not spaghetti code, but almost all subjective, all have pros and cons...

AI makes code review better ))) just need control result.

"𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗲 𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗶𝘀 𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗽𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴𝗹𝘆 𝘀𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲: 𝗶𝘁 𝘀𝗼𝗹𝘃𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺 𝗻𝗼𝘄, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗯𝗲 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗱 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝗿𝗲𝗾𝘂𝗶𝗿𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗳𝘁. That's it. "

I think what you really want to say is that you don't give a damn about code quality. But you know, reading these AI hype texts, it's all too predictable.

I strongly disagree. Difference between good code and bad can be a difference between new developer easily delivering new features and same developer crying and quitting without delivering anything, and hiring process starts again, business progress blocked. "Code works" is merely the most obvious criteria of code quality
Code quality is subjective; business value is objective. This is a great reminder to zoom out from the IDE every once in a while and look at the bottom line.

𝗙𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗮 𝗯𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲, 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗲 𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗶𝘀 𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗽𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴𝗹𝘆 𝘀𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲: 𝗶𝘁 𝘀𝗼𝗹𝘃𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺 𝗻𝗼𝘄, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗯𝗲 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗱 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝗿𝗲𝗾𝘂𝗶𝗿𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗳𝘁. 

I would no use a plane or elevator designed over this premise. They would work 99% of the time and crash 1% of the travels.
What senior dev automatically looks to libraries for help?
Yeah, this hits I’ve seen so many “wrong” code debates that were really just different tradeoffs based on what each dev optimized for at the time.
Which one is getting regular updates and more community engagement? I want that library.
this is exactly how it looks when two AIs, both claiming they’re “the best on the market,” start arguing... 😄
Who told that engeneers think it is bad? :)
Figures why I'm no senior yet.
That's why the Systems Aanalyst exists. S/he gathers the requirements and tells the Senior Devs what to code. S/he is the arbiter of correctness.
Indeed...but if its not maintainable 6 months later...which we've seen...then that's the key
Are you sure you started to use AI since 2018?
As long as they finish arguing before they finish drinking their coffee, this is OK.
Time to re-hire a librarian??

Peter, you're path through life was different to mine. You're argument is mostly correct, but it failed as soon as you said "the rest might just be a matter of taste. 🔧".

Have a good life!
Why did i read all those books on clean coding, pragmatic coding, scrum, waterfall, blah blah blah?
This post is AI slop

The time spent writing proper code will be far lower than the time spent debugging, maintaining and ultimately refactoring poorly written code that introduces a lot of hidden nightmare scenarios. That's at the heart of the problem...you are asking to trade a small extra cost upfront to write good code with a large cost later of fixes, production issues and refactors. In a world of tradeoffs, that's an very, very bad decision. One that is not defensible.

Like "Functional Programming is better Class"...
Also, code might be written 1000 ways...

Yes maintentable code is not spaghetti code, but almost all subjective, all have pros and cons...
